coredata.dc:description: With the increasingly serious problem of water pollution, it is urgent to develop a green and effective pollution-reducing material. This work reported novel biomass carbon quantum dots (BCQDs) prepared based on cat loose hairs, and we combined them with Bi-based photocatalysts. The composite materials BCQDs/Bi2MoO6/Bi2WO6 (BMoW) successfully achieved the effect of rapid degradation of dye wastewater treatment (60 min, degradation rate of 99.9 %). Furthermore, BMoW could be used to treat dyes in different water quality. Interestingly, BMoW was added to the polyacrylamide (PAM) matrix, which could significantly improve the PAM's ability for visual sewage treatment (40 min, degradation rate of 91.1 %). This effect required two steps to achieve: 1. The porous structure of PAM provided physical adsorption; 2. The indirect Z-scheme mechanism of the composite material promoted the chemical degradation process. The indirect Z-scheme mechanism for dye photodegradation with hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide radicals (•O2 −) as the dominant reactive species was also proposed, which was better than other mechanisms for dye photodegradation. This research may provide a new approach to support efficient solutions to the problem of dye degradation in industrial wastewater.

head.ce:title: BCQDs enhanced Bi-based photocatalysts for dye degradation and visual sewage treatment

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for Modification of Chemical Fibers and Polymer Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Donghua University, Shanghai 201620, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Modification of Chemical Fibers and Polymer Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory for Modification of Chemical Fibers and Polymer Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Donghua University, Shanghai, 201620, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the increasingly serious problem of water pollution, it is urgent to develop a green and effective pollution-reducing material. This work reported novel biomass carbon quantum dots (BCQDs) prepared based on cat loose hairs, and we combined them with Bi-based photocatalysts. The composite materials BCQDs/Bi2MoO6/Bi2WO6 (BMoW) successfully achieved the effect of rapid degradation of dye wastewater treatment (60 min, degradation rate of 99.9 %). Furthermore, BMoW could be used to treat dyes in different water quality. Interestingly, BMoW was added to the polyacrylamide (PAM) matrix, which could significantly improve the PAM's ability for visual sewage treatment (40 min, degradation rate of 91.1 %). This effect required two steps to achieve: 1. The porous structure of PAM provided physical adsorption; 2. The indirect Z-scheme mechanism of the composite material promoted the chemical degradation process. The indirect Z-scheme mechanism for dye photodegradation with hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide radicals (•O2 −) as the dominant reactive species was also proposed, which was better than other mechanisms for dye photodegradation. This research may provide a new approach to support efficient solutions to the problem of dye degradation in industrial wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, the increasingly serious problem of water pollution has attracted the attention of researchers. The organic dye wastewater has caused a serious impact on the environment due to its high toxicity and chemical resistance. Photocatalysis technology has become an advanced and effective method to solve the problem of wastewater pollution [1–4]. The photocatalytic oxidation process is thought to work by releasing large amounts of powerful active oxidants such as hydroxyl radicals (•OH), and superoxide ions (•O2 −) which are important in the degradation process. Bismuth-based nanocomposites, such as bismuth tungstate Bi2WO6 (BWO) and bismuth molybdate Bi2MoO6 (BMO) [5], have been widely used in the field of photocatalysis due to their high photocatalytic activity, low toxicity, and high stability [6–8].

The BWO has a narrow band gap, visible light with a long wavelength, and a high specific surface area, which can improve the dyes' degradation [9]. BMO is another Aurivillius perovskite structure semiconductor [10], possessing chemical stability and efficient charge transfer ability due to the [Bi2O2]2+ layered structures and high-quality perovskite [MoO4]2− octahedral crystal layers [11]. However, the BMO generally responds at the ultraviolet wavelength range leading to a low quantum efficiency. In this work, we prepared BMO/BWO (MoW) composite materials. The degradation rate of MoW reached 86.6 % in 60 min, which was almost 3 times that of a single pure substance. Although the MoW demonstrated certain photocatalytic potential, its performance had great space for improvement due to severe carrier recombination, low light capture, and electron transfer ability. Therefore, there is an urgent need to find an effective method for enhancing the MoW photocatalytic performance.

Recently, photocatalytic composites based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a useful strategy for improving charge separation and transfer efficiency [12–16]. CQDs have special advantages of quantum size effect and short carrier transport distance, which are quite important in the field of photocatalysis [17,18]. Particularly, photocatalysts based on biomass CQDs (BCQDs) have received considerable attention due to their environmental protection, high electrical conductivity, and catalytic properties [19–22]. BCQDs, as a good electronic medium, can reduce the recombination of electrons and pore pairs, improving the photocatalytic activity [23–26]. The design of BCQDs-modified MoW heterojunction photocatalysts holds promise for achieving highly efficient photocatalytic performance in pollutant photodegradation. However, the systematic investigation of the reaction mechanisms and the photocatalytic efficiency of BCQD-based heterostructures remains largely unexplored. The material we choose is waste cat hair, which belongs to the effective recycling of green environmental protection. The waste cat hair contains a variety of elements, including C, H, and O, which are needed to synthesize BCQDs.

Herein, we reported novel BCQDs prepared based on cat loose hairs and combined them with Bi-based photocatalysts. The composite materials BCQDs/BMO/BWO (BMoW) successfully achieved an ultra-high degradation rate of 99.9 % in 60 min. BCQDs have good electron storage ability, which can show a synergistic effect in the catalyst, and promote the effective separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. BCQDs act as an intermediary to transfer electrons from BMO to BWO, which means that BMoW can produce an indirect Z-scheme photocatalyst to enhance the photocatalytic degradation of pollutants [27–30].

To enrich the applications of BMoW, we also combined BMoW with the polyacrylamide (PAM) matrix, which could significantly improve the PAM's ability for visual sewage treatment and achieve a high degradation rate of 91.1 % in 40 min. This effect required two steps to achieve: 1. The porous structure of PAM provided physical adsorption; 2. The indirect Z-scheme mechanism of the composite material promoted the chemical degradation process. This research may provide a new approach to support efficient solutions to the problem of dye degradation in industrial wastewater.

Cat hair: collect cat hair cut from pet stores; Ammonium molybdate ((NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O, Shanghai Haohong Biomedical Technology Co., Ltd.); Bismuth nitrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), Sodium tungstate (Na2WO4·2H2O), urea, polyacrylamide (PAM), Rhodamine B (RhB), methylene blue (MB), methyl orange (MO), methyl green (MG), methyl red (MR) and terylene acid (TA) were all purchased from Shanghai Titan Technology Co., Ltd. Nitro tetrazolium chloride blue (NBT, Fuzhou Feijing Biological Technology Co., Ltd.); Sodium hydroxide, potassium dichromate, disodium ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid, ethylene glycol and nitric acid were purchased from Sinopharm Group. All reagents used in this experiment are analytically pure, and the laboratory water is deionized water.

BCQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method [31]. The comparison with other methods for preparing BCQDs was shown in Table S1. 1 g of cat hair was washed thoroughly and dried in the sun. The obtained powder was mixed with distilled water (30 mL), followed by 0.6 g urea, and kept in a high-temperature and high-pressure reactor, where the mixture reacted at 260 °C for 7 h (reaction condition exploration as shown in Figs. S1–3). After the reaction, the brown solution was filtered and cleaned, and the BCQDs solution was obtained in a dialysis bag for 24 h. The Uv-vis and fluorescence spectra of BCQDs are shown in Fig. S4. The diameter of BCQDs was determined by TEM to be about 5.86 nm (Fig. S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation process of xanthium nanoflower structure BMoW
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 5 mmol bismuth nitrate pentahydrate was weighed into 10 mL 2 mol/L nitric acids, the rotation speed of the magnetic stirrer was adjusted, and the mixture was stirred until it was completely dissolved, which was denoted as solution A. Then, 2.5 mmol ammonium molybdate tetrahydrate was weighed into 10 mL 1 mol/L sodium hydroxide solution, denoted as solution B; Solution B was slowly added into solution A after the pH of the mixed solution was controlled with ammonia water, the mixed solution was kept in a high-temperature and high-pressure reactor, reacted at 160 °C for 4 h, cooled to room temperature, and the resulting product was poured into a centrifugal tube, centrifuged at 4000 r for 5 min. Alternately clean three times with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol until neutral. The precursor was obtained after drying at 60 °C for 8 h, and the BMO sample was obtained after roasting at 300 °C for 2 h in a Muffle furnace.

Secondly, 3 mmol bismuth nitrate pentahydrate was weighed into 10 mL glycol, stirred until completely dissolved, and recorded as solution C; Then 1.5 mmol sodium tungstate dihydrate was weighed into 30 mL deionized aqueous solution, denoted as solution D; D solution was slowly added into C solution, a certain amount of BMO and BCQDs solution was added, the mixed solution was kept in a high-temperature and high-pressure reactor, then reacted at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was poured into a centrifugal tube, and centrifuged at 4000 r for 5 min. Alternately cleaned three times with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol, the product was dried at 60 °C for 8 h to obtain BMoW nanocomposites. Finally, the structural characterization of all the prepared samples was tested.

1.5 g PAM and a certain amount of (0 g, 0.079 g, 0.167 g, 0.265 g, 0.375 g, 0.5 g) BMoW powder was weighed into 30 mL water, stirred until completely mixed, then transferred the mixture to the grinding tool. The composite aerogel (p-BMoW) with different proportions (0 %, 5 %, 10 %, 15 %, 20 %, 25 %) was prepared by freeze-drying for 5 h (The detailed preparation chart is shown in Fig. 1 ).

The functional groups of BMoW were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR; Nicolet iS20, Thermo Scientific, USA). The chemical structure and composition of BMoW were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD; Ultima IV, Rigaku, Japan). The size and morphology of BMoW prepared by scanning electron microscope (SEM; Sigma 300, ZEISS, Germany) and transmission electron microscope (TEM; JEM-2100F, JEOL, Japan); Ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (UV–Vis; GENESYS 50, Thermo Fisher, USA) records UV–Vis spectrum; The optical properties of the catalyst were investigated by solid UV–Vis (UV–Vis; UV3600, Shimadzu, Japan) diffuse reflectance spectroscopy. Photocatalytic instrument (PLS-SXE300, electric current: 18 A).

Under visible light, the photocatalytic activity of the prepared material was evaluated by various pollutants, using a 300 W Xe lamp as a light source. The 15 mg photocatalyst was dispersed respectively in MB, RhB, MO, MG, and MR Solutions (50 mL, 10 mg/L). After achieving an adsorption-desorption equilibrium in the dark, the solution was irradiated at 25 °C for 60 min. During the illumination process, 3 mL of the reaction solution was extracted at a certain interval and filtered with a 0.22 μm filter head. The dye absorbance was determined by a GenESYS50 UV-VIS spectrophotometer.

p-BMoW was added into MB solution (50 mL, 10 mg/L). After achieving an adsorption-desorption equilibrium in the dark, the solution was irradiated at 25 °C for 40 min. During the illumination process, 3 mL of the reaction solution was extracted at a certain interval and filtered with a 0.22 μm filter head. The dye absorbance was determined by a GenESYS50 UV-VIS spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of BCQDs and composite materials
ce:section.ce:para: The crystalline nature and phase information of prepared samples were showcased in the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns (Fig. 2a). According to the standard XRD card of BWO (JCPDS.39–0256), the pure BWO diffraction peaks at 28.2°,32.9°,46.9°,55.8° and 58.6°were assigned to (131), (200), (260), (331) and (262) crystal planes of BWO respectively, and no other impurity peaks appeared. Likewise, the pure BMO diffraction peaks at 28.2°,32.5°,47.2°,55.4°, and 58.5°were assigned to (131), (002), (260), (133), and (262) crystal faces of BMO (JCPDS.21–0102) respectively. BCQDs had a diffraction peak at 24.5°, indicating the amorphous character of BCQDs. We found that in BMoW composite materials: For the BMO characteristic peak, the FWHM slightly increases and the crystallite size decreases; For the BWO characteristic peak, the FWHM slightly decreases and the crystallite size increases [32]. Based on XPS data, BMoW has a lower content of the Mo element and a higher content of the W element. Therefore, the crystallinity of BWO crystals increases during the composite process, while the crystallinity of BMO crystals decreases during the composite process [33](Tables S2–4). Because the number of BCQDs added was relatively small, and BCQDs had non-crystalline characteristics, BCQDs did not appear as diffraction peaks on the XRD of composite materials. Fourier transforms infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed to further confirm the internal chemical structure of the catalyst (Fig. 2b). The OH stretching vibration peaks of adsorbed water were observed at 3436 cm−1 and 1632 cm−1 for all samples. The absorption bands of 811 cm−1 and 738 cm−1 were aroused by the stretching and bending vibrations of Bi⁃O and W/Mo⁃O bonds [34] respectively. In addition, we could find that with the decrease of the proportion of BMO in the BMoW, the peak intensity at 1082 cm−1 also decreased. There was an anti-symmetric stretching vibrational peak attributed to CH in BCQDs located at 2922 cm−1. The characteristic peaks belonging to BCQDs were observed in the BMoW composites, without detecting other impurity peaks. Therefore, it could be concluded that the BMoW composites were successfully prepared.

SEM and TEM were used to characterize the microstructure and nanostructure of the photocatalyst. The BWO was composed of a nanoflower spherical structure with a diameter of about 2.5 μm, and the morphology of the products was uniform (Fig. 3a). The BMO was a nanosheet structure with uniform thickness and smooth surface (Fig. 3b). In addition, the BMoW showed a 4.5 μm xanthium nanoflower-coated sheet structure (Fig. 3c). Fig. 3d showed that the lattice spacing of BCQDs was 0.2 nm, corresponding to the graphite (100) crystal face, and HRTEM test further revealed the internal structure and lattice of the nanocomposite, the lattice fringes of 0.274 nm were the (002) crystal faces of BMO, 0.165 nm was the (331) crystal face of BWO and 0.209 nm was the (100) crystal face of BCQDs (Fig. 3e). The results showed that the BMoW composites were successfully prepared by hydrothermal method. It could be seen from the EDS (Fig. 3f-k) that the elements comprising the BMoW include Bi, C, Mo, O, and W, which further indicated that the nanosphere-coated sheet BMoW was successfully prepared in this experiment.

Furthermore, the elemental existence and chemical state of Bi, Mo, C, O, and W were revealed by XPS survey spectra (Fig. 4a). The distributed peaks in Bi 4f spectra represented at Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 respectively, which were located at 159.5 and 164.8 eV (Fig. 4b), It showed the surface chemical element of Bi existed as Bi metal.[35] Moreover, the two peaks at 35.7 and 37.9 eV of BMoW samples were associated with W 4f7/2 and W 4f5/2 [36], respectively (Fig. 4c). In Fig. 4d, the two main peaks at 232.32 and 235.43 eV corresponded to the binding energy of Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 [37] respectively. In Fig. 4e, the peak at 284.8 eV was the CC skeleton peak, while at 286.1 and 288.2 eV were the peak of the C⁃C and CO bonds in BCQDs [38]; The chromatographic peak of O1s at 530 eV was the peak of BiO [39] (Fig. 4f). The XPS spectra of BWO, BMO, and BCQDs were shown in the Fig. S6. Those phenomena proved the stable structure and combined chemical bonds of BMoW.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic properties of BCQDs and composite materials

ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 5a, BMoW exhibited higher MB degradation efficiency (99.9 %) than BWO (45.3 %), BMO (27.5 %), and MoW (86.6 %). The photodegradation of pure BCQDs (p-BCQDs) and the comparison of three materials are shown in Fig. S7. The photocatalytic degradation rate of pure MB is shown in Fig. S8.

To further optimize the catalytic condition, the effects of catalyst dosages (0–0.5 g/L) and initial MB concentrations (10–50 mg/L) were investigated. The dosage of the catalyst had a positive effect on the catalytic reaction (Fig. 5b). Within the scope of 0–0.3 g/L catalyst dosage, the degradation efficiency increased sharply as the catalyst increased. From actual comprehensive benefits, the catalyst dosage of 0.3 g/L was an economical option. Meanwhile, the high initial concentration of MB also affected the photodegradation reaction (Fig. 5c). It made it hard for photons to reach the reaction interface of the catalyst, and the photoactive species were reduced [40]. We determined 10 mg/L as the initial concentration of MB. The MB degradation rate under different pH (pH =3–11) was illustrated (Fig. 5d). The effect of co-existing ions on the photocatalytic performance has been added in Fig. S8. We found that Cl− and CO3 2− don‘t have much effect on the photocatalytic degradation performance, but SO4 2− could reduce it (Fig. S9). The results showed that BMoW exhibited uniform behavior at all pH levels. BMoW was an outstanding photocatalyst for MB degradation in a wide range of pH environments. The optimal environmental condition was pH =7. We supplemented the zeta potential of BMoW and calculated the zero charge point by fitting curves based on different pH values (Fig. S10).

We carried out five photocatalytic cycle experiments on BMoW photocatalyst. Fig. 6a showed that after the 5 times cycling experiments, the MB degradation efficiency was still as high as 99.4 %. The long-term stability study of BMoW is shown in Fig. S11.

To understand the ability of BMoW photocatalysts to degrade various dyes, RhB, MO, MG, and MR dyes were used as experimental subjects. In Fig. 6b-e, the degradation rates of these dyes were 94.1 %, 85.3 %, 87.4 %, and 84.5 %, respectively. It reflected the extensive use of BMoW photocatalysts (Fig. 6f), the degradation color changes being shown in Fig. 6g. In summary, BMoW demonstrated high catalytic efficiency and wide applicability compared to previous studies (Table 1 ).

To verify the effectiveness of the BMoW catalyst in wastewater treatment, the MB degradation rates were performed in water from three different natural sources: tap water, lake water, and river water (Fig. 7a-c). The degradation rates of BMoW in these water sources were 98.1 %, 94.3 %, and 91.7 % within 60 min. These differences are not significant compared to the degradation rates in deionized water (99.9 %).

In addition, we selected lake water with a moderate degradation rate for further study on the effect of concentration, as shown in Fig. 8a and Fig. S12a-e. The MB concentration in lake water was inversely proportional to the degradation rate, which was consistent with the law in deionized water (Fig. 8b). This indicated that the photocatalyst could be used to treat wastewater with different pollution levels.

The electron transfer mechanism was an important link in explaining the reaction mechanism. To study the charge conveying pathways in depth, the band structure needed to be calculated. Therefore, the UV–visible diffuse reflection results were referenced to obtain the band gaps (Eg) of BWO, BMO, BCQDs, and BMoW. Where the direct transition (indirect transition) in the semiconductor photocatalyst, the value of n was 2 (or 0.5), here for BMO and BWO, n = 2 [55]. The optical band gap energy of BMO (2.63 eV), BWO (2.75 eV), BCQDs (2.17 eV), and BMoW (1.85 eV) was determined by linearly extrapolated Tauc plots (Fig. 9a). The band gap energy of the composites reduced significantly due to the BCQDs, indicating the improved optical performance.

In addition, the flat band potential (Ef) of BMO and BWO could be seen from Mott-Schottky diagram modulation as −0.08 and − 0.95 V and (compared to Ag/AgCl) (Fig. 9b-c). From the positive slope of the C−2 curve, BMO and BWO were both N-type semiconductors (Eq. (1) [55–57]). So the BMoW was also the N-type semiconductor, through testing of photocurrent and resistance, we found that BMoW is an anodic photocurrent material and an N-type semiconductor (Fig. S13). Therefore, the conduction potentials of BMO and BWO were finally determined to be −0.28 and − 1.15 eV, and the valence potentials of BMO and BWO were 2.41 and 1.6 eV.
Formula (1)
Conduction band potential of
N − type semiconductor = Flat band potential − 0.2

0.2
is the experience value

Above this, the zonal structure of the BMoW heterojunction was proved to be an indirect Z-scheme heterojunction [58] (Fig. 9d). The KPFM image of BMoW was shown in Fig. S14, which proved the formation and flow of electrons. After being irradiated by visible light, BCQDs could enhance the excitation effect by receiving visible light to produce electron pore pairs. BMO and BWO also produced light-generating electrons and holes on their CB (Conduction Band) and VB (Valence Band), respectively. In addition, BCQDs not only acted as an electronic medium, transferring electrons from CB in BMO to VB in BWO, but bound with the holes present there, resulting in a reduction in electron-hole recombination in BWO and BMO. As a result, CB of BWO and VB of BMO produced extremely high electron and pore yields, respectively. Since the CB position of BWO (−1.15 eV) was more negative than the O2/•O2 − REDOX potential (−0.28 eV), electrons could react with dissolved oxygen in water to form •O2 − free radicals. Similarly, the VB position of BMO (2.41 eV) was more corrected than the REDOX potential of the H2O/•OH pair (2.27 eV), and the pores on the VB of BMO could react with water to produce •OH radicals [25], which could promote the oxidation properties of the dye, thus causing its color changing.

As mentioned above, BCQDs could act as an electron reservoir and acceptor during photocatalytic reactions due to the presence of the banded structure system, accelerating charge migration and preventing electron pore pairs from recombining. The comparison of dye degradation ability with other Z-type heterojunction photocatalysts is shown in Table S5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of active species during photocatalysis
ce:section.ce:para:
In the process of photocatalytic MB degradation, Nitrobluetet⁃razolium (NBT) was used as a sacrifice agent to capture the active species superoxide radical (·O2 −) produced during the photocatalytic process, NBT had a characteristic absorption peak at 259 nm. When it reacted with ·O2 − to produce bluish-purple Formazan precipitation [60], the absorption peak of NBT at 259 nm decreased, so it could be inferred whether ·O2 − was produced in the photocatalytic process according to the change curve of NBT (the specific reaction equation of NBT and ·O2 − was shown in Eq. (1) [60]). According to the comparison of the NBT absorption peak intensity of BMO, BWO, MoW, and BMoW, BMoW photocatalyst produced a large amount of ·O2 − (Fig. 11 ), and thus had the best photocatalytic performance.
(1)

The principle of reaction of Terephthalic acid (TA) and ·OH to form 2⁃hydroxyl terephthalic acid (TAOH) was used to detect the hydroxyl radical (·OH) produced in the photocatalysis process (the specific reaction equation was shown in Eq. (2)). In the fluorescence (PL) spectrum, TAOH had strong fluorescence at 400 nm when the excitation wavelength was 315 nm, and the amount of ·OH produced in the photocatalysis process could be judged according to the strength of the PL peak. Under visible illumination conditions, with BMO, BWO, MoW, and BMoW as catalysts, the fluorescence peak intensity of TAOH increased with the extension of illumination time, indicating that hydroxyl free radicals were generated by these four photocatalysts (Fig. 12 a-d). Among them, the PL peak strength of TAOH produced by BMoW was higher than that of BMO, BWO, and MoW, which further verified that BCQDs doping could effectively improve the photocatalytic activity of nanocomposites.
(2)

To further determine the generation of active free radicals by BMoW under visible light irradiation, we adopted EPR electron spin capture technology. Four characteristic peaks of the DMPO-•OH species were observed at 1:2:2:1 intensity, and the peak intensity was maintained after 10 min of irradiation (Fig. 13 a). At the same time, the characteristic peak of DMPO-•O2 − was also produced after light irradiation (Fig. 13b). These results confirmed that BMoW could produce •OH and •O2 − free radicals under visible light irradiation, which participated in the REDOX reaction and played the role of degrading organic pollutants in the water.

PAM has made good progress in the field of flocculant decontamination presently [61]. BMoW could degrade five dyes: MB, RhB, MO, MG, and MR. Our work combined PAM with BMoW to prepare p-BMoW, which had good performance in degrading dye wastewater. In the part of dyes, MB, which had the best degradation performance among the five dyes, was selected for display. In Fig. 14 a-f, the photocatalytic degradation rates of 0 %, 5 %, 10 %, 15 %, 20 %, and 25 % p-BMoW were 43.3 %, 66.4 %, 75.1 %, 81.2 %, 87.8 % and 91.1 %, respectively. We also found that 30 % p-BMoW and above will cause serious agglomeration, affecting the photocatalytic performance, so we chose a gradient of 0–25 % p-BMoW, as shown in Fig. S15. As the doping amount of BMoW photocatalyst increased, the adsorption and degradation performance of PAM could be improved in a short time. We also degraded the MB in water from three different natural sources: Tap water, lake water, and river water (Fig. 14 g-i), the degradation rates of 25 % p-BMoW within 40 min were 88.8 %, 86.2 %, and 82.1 %, which showed little difference compared with the degradation rates in deionized water (91.1 %). The color change of 25 % p-BMoW degraded dye in deionized water was shown in Fig. 14k. Therefore, the water source had little impact on the performance of p-BMoW photocatalytic degradation of dye wastewater, which indicated that p-BMoW could be used for wastewater treatment of various water sources.

The degradation mechanism of p-BMoW could be roughly divided into two processes: physical adsorption and chemical degradation. First, p-BMoW was put into water, because of its hole structure on the surface (Fig. 15 a, Fig. S13a), it could adsorb dye molecules and BMoW molecules on it, the pore size of p-BMoW is about 30.94 μm (Fig. S12). It could be seen from the EDS mapping (Fig. S17 b-g) that the elements comprising the p-BMoW include C, N, O, Mo, W, and Bi. The XPS spectra of p-BMoW were shown in Fig. S18, such as the peak of W metal (34.8 and 37.0 eV), Mo metal (232.1 and 234.6 eV), and CO, etc., which further indicated that the p-BMoW was successfully prepared in this experiment. The Eg of p-BMoW (1.85 eV) was determined by linearly extrapolated Tauc plots (Fig. S19), which was the same as BMoW's (1.85 eV) (Fig. 9a). Therefore, the composite of PAM did not affect the photocatalytic performance of BMoW, and due to BCQD, the bandgap energy of the composite material was significantly reduced, indicating an improvement in catalytic performance.

Then we carried out 40 min light reaction due to the existence of the BMoW. The CB of BWO and the VB of BMO generated extremely high electron and pore yields respectively. Electrons could react with dissolved oxygen in the water to form •O2 − radicals; Pores could react with water to produce •OH free radicals and these active groups promoted the oxidation properties of dyes (Fig. 15b), resulting in changes in their color.

In summary, the BMoW nanocomposites modified with biomass carbon quantum dots were successfully prepared by hydrothermal method, and the results showed that BMoW was a xanthium nanoflower indirect heterojunction structure. BWO combined with BMO constructed a suitable band structure with high REDOX capacity. BCQDs contributed to the production and separation of more light-induced electrons and pores. We confirmed the optimal reaction conditions for BMoW at pH =7, low dosage (0.3 g/L), and dye concentration (10 mg/L). The synthesized nanocomposite had an excellent MB photocatalytic degradation rate, and the degradation rate reached 99.9 % within 60 min of ultraviolet irradiation. We also found that BMoW still maintained 72.95 % in 60 min after seven days. Then, the reaction mechanism of this photocatalytic process was demonstrated by the charge transfer mechanism and the generation of active free radicals. Finally, the composite aerogel prepared by the composite material and PAM was applied to the visual sewage treatment, and the degradation rate reached 91.1 % within 40 min of ultraviolet irradiation. Therefore, this cost-effective nanocomposite material synthesized had strong potential in effectively removing dyes from industrial wastewater.

Meiheng Gan: Writing – original draft, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Zhengkun Ma: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Wenting Zhang: Resources. Xiaoxuan Qie: Investigation. Shilin Liu: Writing – review & editing. Jingzong He: Writing – review & editing. Qilin Wu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.
